concept_name: Shortest-Time River Crossing — Aim Straight Across
source_book: DC Pandey 6.11 — minimum-time crossing strategy; split from river_boat_problems

real_world_anchor.primary: A Kolkata ferry pilot crossing the Hooghly from Howrah Station ghat to Fairlie Place. The river is 500 m wide and flows south at 2 m/s. His boat can do 3 m/s in still water. If he wants to reach the other bank in the LEAST time, he points the bow PERPENDICULAR to the current, straight across. He will drift downstream while crossing — landing south of Fairlie Place — but the crossing time is 500 / 3 = 167 seconds, the minimum possible. Every Kolkata ferry does this at rush hour.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A monsoon rescue boat on the flooded Yamuna. The medical team needs to cross 80 m to an island stranded with patients; the river is rushing at 3 m/s, their boat does 4 m/s still-water. They aim straight across — minimum time is 80 / 4 = 20 seconds. Drift is acceptable because another team waits downstream to catch them.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A swimmer on a Goa tourist beach during a rip current. She has 40 m of open water to reach the shore. The rip pulls her parallel to the beach at 1.5 m/s; she swims at 1 m/s in still water. Standard advice: swim PERPENDICULAR to the rip — shortest time to clear the rip zone — and worry about landing position afterward.

formulas.t_min: t_min  =  width / v_boat   (boat pointed straight across, full boat-velocity serves the crossing)
formulas.drift_downstream: drift_during_crossing  =  v_river * t_min  =  v_river * width / v_boat
formulas.landing_angle_from_perpendicular: tan(theta)  =  v_river / v_boat   (drift angle of resulting ground path)

constraints: The boat's heading (where the bow points) is perpendicular to the banks.
The ground path is DIAGONAL because of the river drift — NOT straight across.
Minimum time always occurs when the full v_boat contributes to the across-direction.
Minimum time is independent of v_river — the current only changes WHERE you land, not WHEN.

STATE_1.title: The Strategy — Point the Bow Straight Across

scene_composition.text:
Hooghly river at Kolkata:

    Howrah ghat       ||-----------||       far bank
                                                     
                                                     
                                                     
    width = 500 m            river flows south at 2 m/s  ->

   Boat still-water speed  v_boat  =  3 m/s
   River current           v_river =  2 m/s

   Goal: cross in LEAST time.
   Trick: point the bow STRAIGHT at the far bank (perpendicular).

    t_min = width / v_boat = 500 / 3 = 167 s  (~ 2 minutes 47 s)

   The full 3 m/s now works for the crossing.  Nothing is wasted.
Kolkata ferry: Howrah -> Fairlie Place ghat

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the counter-intuitive strategy every Kolkata ferry pilot knows.
If you want to cross the river in the shortest TIME, you point the bow STRAIGHT ACROSS, not upstream.
Why? Because all three metres per second of boat speed now go into the crossing direction. Nothing is wasted fighting the current.
Crossing time is width divided by boat speed — 500 divided by 3 — 167 seconds. Just under three minutes.

scene_composition.text:
The downside: you do not land opposite.

   During 167 s, the river carries you downstream at 2 m/s.

      drift  =  v_river * t_min
             =  2  *  167
             =  334 m downstream

   You LAND 334 m south of Fairlie Place,
   somewhere near Babughat ferry point.

   The actual ground path is DIAGONAL:

        Howrah   .
                 .
                   .
                      .
                         . <- drifted landing
        far bank ----------||

   The bow always points across (perpendicular).
   The ground track is angled.

tts_sentences.text_en: There is a price for this strategy. You drift downstream while crossing.
At 2 metres per second of current, in 167 seconds of crossing, the boat moves 334 metres south of the intended landing.
So you land well south of Fairlie Place — near Babughat, in fact.
The bow is pointing perpendicular, but the actual track over the ground is a sloping diagonal. Two different directions at the same time.

STATE_3.title: When is Drift Acceptable? When It Isn't?

scene_composition.text:
When shortest-TIME crossing is the right choice:

    * Medical emergency
    * Clearing a rip-current zone (swimmer safety)
    * Surveying, as long as a pickup vehicle follows downstream
    * Navy exercises or rescue ops

   When shortest-TIME is a BAD choice:

    * You must reach a specific dock (banking for boarding)
    * There is a dangerous obstacle downstream
    * A long drift kills the mission (fuel, tides)

   For those cases you need shortest-PATH crossing,
   which is a separate problem.
When is Drift Acceptable? When It Isn't?
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: When is this shortest-time strategy the right one?
Medical emergencies, rip-current escapes, navy rescue ops — anywhere speed matters more than landing position.
It is the WRONG strategy when you must dock at a specific ghat, or when something dangerous lies downstream.
For those cases, you instead aim the bow UPSTREAM at an angle and take a slower but straight-across path — that is the shortest-PATH problem.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student assumes you should aim upstream to 'fight the current', thinking that is the fastest way to cross.
confusion_patterns: point bow upstream for minimum time

scene_composition.text:
Student's logic:
  "The current pushes me downstream.
   If I aim my boat UPSTREAM,
   I neutralise the current and cross faster."

  Appealing, but wrong.

  When you aim upstream, only part of the boat's 3 m/s
  goes across the river; the rest fights the current.
  The result: across-component becomes SMALLER,
  so crossing time goes UP.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Student's reasoning sounds clever: if the current pushes me downstream, let me aim upstream to cancel it out and cross faster.
The instinct is wrong.
When you tilt the bow upstream, some of your 3 metres per second fights the current instead of driving the crossing.
The across-component of velocity becomes smaller — so the crossing takes LONGER, not shorter.

scene_composition.text:
Split the boat velocity into components:

  Bow angle upstream of perpendicular = theta

  Across-component = v_boat * cos(theta)
  Upstream-component = v_boat * sin(theta)

  Crossing time  =  width / (v_boat * cos(theta))

  To MINIMISE that time:
     make cos(theta) as LARGE as possible
     -> theta = 0  (bow perpendicular)
     -> crossing time = width / v_boat

  Any tilt upstream (theta > 0) shrinks cos(theta)
  and INCREASES crossing time. Math agrees with nature.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Let the math decide. Split v boat into an across-component and an upstream-component.
Across-component is v boat times cosine theta. Crossing time is width divided by that component.
To minimise, make cosine as large as possible. Cosine hits its maximum of 1 when theta equals zero — bow perpendicular.
Any upstream tilt shrinks cosine and makes the crossing slower. The math agrees with what every ferry pilot already knows.

scene_composition.text:
Remember these two lines forever:

  Shortest TIME:
     Bow perpendicular. Drift downstream, land elsewhere.

  Shortest PATH (straight across):
     Bow tilted upstream at an angle. Slower crossing.

  They are different optimisations. Same boat, different goal.

  The word in the question matters:
     'shortest time'  -> perpendicular bow
     'shortest path'  -> tilted bow
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Burn these two into memory — they are tested in every JEE river question.
Shortest TIME crossing, bow perpendicular, drift allowed.
Shortest PATH crossing, bow tilted upstream, slower but straight across.
Read the question carefully. The ONE word that matters — time or path — decides which formula to use.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Belief: Aim Upstream For Min Time (auto-generated from branch_id — needs author rewrite).
